speaker: interviewer
text: Of an interview with Simon Stemer on November 13, 1995. We are going to go back a little bit to after the war to find out what happened to your family and how did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was living in this home in Lincolnwood. Very nice home. We live very comfort. And my-- Friday, my wife took my son to the barber for a haircut. She went to pick him up, she got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wife's name was [INAUDIBLE] The family name is [NON-ENGLISH]. She have three sisters, one brother. They all-- two sisters were married. They have children, grandchildren. From the other side, nobody left. Only she was, my wife, [INAUDIBLE], she was my girlfriend before the war. We was thinking to get married. Meantime, they start the war. Talking about a war, we don't married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I-- when I was liberated, after the liberation, is everybody-- young people they grabbed the girls, boys. They get married. I don't know. I was thinking-- I was hoping maybe she is alive. I was hoping. And I was not in a hurry. Back in the place what I live over there, was-- was over there a girl. I don't remember her name. This-- this man from the apartment said, maybe you marry this girl? Maybe you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. I don't feel right to marry somebody. I don't know if I will be a good husband. If I will still think from the other one. I have to wait. I wait. She was liberated in May. And I was already in this time, yeah, before she comed home-- and she was in Majdanek. And from Majdanek they send a transport from [INAUDIBLE] to some other camp. Meantime, they stop off someplace. She and a girlfriend went out from the train, and they went in-- in a home what is first with a German, [INAUDIBLE] rank [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And they was working over there at the time, from the-- the-- from the wartime. They come from the police, asking her how they come. She told that they went. She don't said she went in the transport. From Majdanek, she said. She went with a transport. Maybe they went out for coffee, and the transport left. They was working over there, but from the [GERMAN] apartment till the war end. Later, they was over there. Only she went-- same thing to her town. She don't find nobody. She went to this town, [INAUDIBLE]. This was five kilometer from that town. She find over there some people from her town, and they told her I'm alive. They know I'm alive. She give me a telegram, and I come right. And we get married. In the same week, we get married, and we come back to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family, to your parents? What happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents? My father passed away in 1916 when I was seven years old. My mother was, after the war in 1918, opened up a little grocery store. And we were still living. We was not rich. According to living in-- in Poland, we-- we have the-- for Saturday we have fish and we have meat. We have a chicken. They call us rich. We live over there comfort. And my brother, he got married. I work in the store, and one brother was working for, like, a painter. And my sister, she cannot get high education. Only she was in the night-- the college, and she got a degree. And she was working [INAUDIBLE]. And the other sister, my mother send her to get lectures to make brassieres. She bought a machine, and she was working. She was making nice money. Only this was, the war broke out. It's very bad-- very bad for the people to be-- to be in the wartime. And the time was different life. This what we decide to go to Ludmir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your mother during the war and to your-- your sisters and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother left, when me-- went with my two sisters to Ludmir. My one sister went to Lodz. She got a job from the government. And my sister, she was-- that's in Ludmir. Mother wrote a letter to come home. She's alone. She left Ludmir. It was, in this time, was a commit in Ludmir what they send back the Polish people to-- to Germany because this was the Russian-- the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was living with her mother later. We don't hear nothing till what-- really, in the wartime. Nobody know it what is going on with this killing. They're living-- they're living, when I saw it with my own eyes, when I saw it in 1943-- what happened? 1941. It was until later, we was thinking maybe they got better like in the Russia. You never know. How did you find out what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the war, when I was liberated from Wlodzimierz, I went to Krasnystaw. And I don't have nobody over there. I cannot find nobody. The older Polish people, what I know over there, they said they don't know. They don't know. They don't know. And they were-- they don't like the Jews even. I went back to Ludmir. And later when they won and I was back in Ludmir and I find out what happened everything, to the-- to the family, what happened. I know they took them to Sobibor. In 22 days in a year, in 1942, they all got liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who-- who was taken to Sobibor? Who was taken to Sobibor?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Who was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole town was take in cart and with wagons to Sobibor, and they went right in the gas chamber. They don't-- over there, they don't need-- In other camps, they sent people to work. Over there, they send them right to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war and you married your wife, you had two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my daughter was born in Germany. She born the 22nd of October, 1946 in Germany. And my son was born August 6, 1948 in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son have four kids. My daughter, she got married, and she-- she moved to San Francisco. And she got divorced. Now, she is married again. No children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever tell your children your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. When I come to my son, is the-- I tell the kids my story. One of my granddaughter wrote already two composition for the school, and she got very good marks. I have the articles someplace, what she wrote about the Grandpa, their life, and what happened to the whole family. She wrote two compositions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your grandchildren know also your story? Your grandchildren know what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one granddaughter, she wrote-- the 14-year-old granddaughter, she wrote about a year ago. She-- she wrote for the school, the report.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What I want to ask you now, is there anything that you want to add to your story that I did not ask you before? Is there anything else you would like to add to your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like to add in the story, life in the United State is different like life in Europe. The people in United State, they don't realize it-- what is called good life. For me, because I-- I saw-- I was in the wartime in different place. I was in [INAUDIBLE]. I was in Latvia. I was in Russia. I was in Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is no place was people live so good like they live in United State. I'm just wondering if-- why isn't United State communism? Communism is a thing what they want to take away from the rich and give the poor. I think here is everybody rich. Have a nice home. Have two car. Have a garage. Have money in the bank.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't understand what they want with the Communism. They had to be-- they have to be living Europe. They can't appreciate it what it is United State. They cannot-- they cannot complain to take away the money from the rich. Everybody's working. Everybody's making a good living. You go downtown. You see people walking. You go entertaining, you see people. You go to an opera, you see people living, existing. And-- and special for our Jews. It's no country was-- but Jews cannot understand this life, how good this is in this country. If Jews are thinking in some countries is better, this is wrong. Because usual in this place what you live, if I live in Vencent Tower and if I talked to in a person from 300, he said, Vincent Tower have better people. I take the same people in Vincent Tower and the same people in 300 and the other buildings. You have to live. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I live in Vincent Tower 10 years. I don't have fight with nobody. I have all met friend, and I don't know why people have complaining they have bad neighbors. What I have to my neighbors? I cannot complain. To the whole country, I cannot say something wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- in my age, if I was free in Europe, my grandpa lived to 78 years old. He was, at a young age, he was in the wheat business. Only later when his wife passed away, he stayed by his son. It was-- it was not like here to have social security, also have old age home. He lived by his son, by the older son [INAUDIBLE]. He have two rooms. His bed was by the door. In winter, the door was frozen going outside, and it was cold and was freezing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here, United State, the older people, they have social security. They have checks coming in from different kind stock. They living good. What is to complain? If somebody want to go in an old age home, he is living good. He have money to pay.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Europe, was very bad for the old people. If-- if-- if I go to my doctor and if I-- I tell him, I am just so happy to be in United States in the old age. I don't have to ask the kids. My son told me two years ago, Daddy, we have a big apartment. We have eight rooms, five wash rooms, with a swimming pool, with a finished up basement. He asked me two years ago, Daddy, maybe you will stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I ask him, Alex, what I will do here? You live in [INAUDIBLE], 25 miles from the city. You have a woman. You have a baby in the house. You have a woman in the house. What I will do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I'm living. I pay rent. I pay $675 for an apartment. I live comfort. I have once a week coming in a woman. I want, I go hear a symphony. If I want to go see a movie-- if I'm still be able to go shopping. I have a woman cleaning. It's no complain. It's a very good life for the people here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I want to thank you very much for sharing your testimony with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my uncle [? Sthrul ?] Lerman. He was a very high educated scholar. The place is in Krasnystaw. The town is Krasnystaw. He make this picture 1935. Send it to his son to Chicago-- he lived in Chicago-- from Poland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my aunt [INAUDIBLE]. This is the wife of [? Sthrul ?] Lerman. This is the mother of my cousin Liep Lerman. This was-- was taken in Krasnystaw, and the date, 1935. This picture was taken in Berlin, in Germany. My daughter when she was born, 1946, October 22nd in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is with you daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- this my wife and me. We took a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- being in the [SPEAKING GERMAN] in 1946 till '47. They-- being over there, they give you room and board and food until we emigrate to-- to USA, to United State. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my daughter Rosalie and my son Alex and a girlfriend. They went to the Hebrew school in Chicago. And this was celebrating-- they was the cantors for the holiday for [? Rosh Hashanah ?] and Yom Kippur. This was in 1956. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the son and the man what they kept me seven months. His name was Leon Gatcinski. The son was [INAUDIBLE] Gatcinski, and he bring us the food to the bunker. I've was over there in 1982 with my daughter. We took the picture. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This picture is from my son and my daughter-in-law and the four grandchildren. The name of the grandchildren is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Andy and-- and Emily and Joyce and Jack. They-- we-- I was over there in-- in Chicago. They live in Munster, Indiana, and they took the picture. This was taken in 1981, yeah.
